title: Statement on the Release of the Tri-party Repo Infrastructure Reform Task Force's Final Report

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York issued a statement on February 15, 2012 responding to the Tri-party Repo Infrastructure Reform Task Force's final report, acknowledging progress in operational changes but noting that systemic risk in the tri-party repo market remained unchanged because reliance on intraday credit from clearing banks had not been meaningfully reduced. The Fed announced it would intensify direct oversight of clearing banks and FICC and explore additional policy options including collateral restrictions and an industry-financed liquidation facility.
key_points: The tri-party repo market exhibited structural weaknesses exposed during the 2008 financial crisis that required extraordinary Fed policy actions to prevent market collapse

The Task Force identified three areas of concern: overreliance on intraday credit from clearing banks, procyclical risk management practices, and absence of orderly liquidation process for defaulted broker-dealers
Market participants implemented automated collateral substitution and 3-way trade confirmation processes, but the 2011 deadline to complete recommended operational changes was not achieved
Intraday credit provided by clearing banks has not yet been meaningfully reduced, leaving systemic risk unchanged
At least one clearing bank was expected to eliminate non-maturing trades from the daily unwind process later in 2012
The NY Fed will intensify direct oversight of infrastructure changes and explore additional policy options including collateral restrictions and an industry-financed liquidation facility
why_it_matters: The tri-party repo market is critical financing infrastructure for the U.S. financial system. This statement signals regulatory commitment to addressing systemic vulnerabilities that contributed to the 2008 crisis, specifically the system's dependence on intraday credit from two clearing banks (Bank of New York Mellon and JPMorgan Chase). Market participants—dealers, borrowers, and investors—need to prepare for multi-year infrastructure changes and potential policy interventions that could affect how repo financing is structured and collateralized.
macro_context: This 2012 statement documents the Federal Reserve's initial framework for addressing tri-party repo market vulnerabilities exposed during the 2008 financial crisis. While subsequent reforms (including eventual implementation of many recommended changes) have modified market structure since then, this document provides essential historical context for understanding the evolution of U.S. repo market infrastructure and the regulatory approach to systemic risk reduction in short-term funding markets.
market_structure_context: The tri-party repo market involves two clearing banks (Bank of New York Mellon and JPMorgan Chase) that settle repo transactions between borrowers (primarily broker-dealers) and lenders (money market funds, banks, and other investors). The core structural issue is market participants' reliance on intraday credit from these clearing banks, which creates systemic risk in stressed environments. The Fed is requiring infrastructure changes including automated collateral substitution, elimination of non-maturing trades from the daily unwind, and improvements to the collateral liquidation process for defaulted dealers.
